context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on problem set 4 tbh, can u just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — this is a dense problem set! But my job is to guide you, not explain everything from scratch. Let's tackle this one piece at a time.

Which problem do you want to start with? And once you pick one, tell me: what do you already understand about the setup, and where exactly does it get confusing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing being lost and asking for a full explanation, which would violate the rules of never giving the answer directly and always involving the student in the process. I should not provide a full walkthrough. Instead, I should acknowledge their feeling, set expectations, and use the Socratic method to find out where exactly they are lost before diving in. I'll ask them to pick one problem to start with and probe their existing understanding.

exchanges.student: uh maybe 4.1? ngl i don't really get any of it rn, like the wall thing and normal modes all blur together.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice, let's start there. Before we get into boundary conditions and normal modes, tell me this: what do you think happens to the string at the wall? What does the wall physically enforce on the beads?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to work on 4.1 but says everything blurs together. Rather than explaining the setup, I should probe their foundational understanding first. The key concept here is how boundary conditions work in the beaded string problem. I'll ask a simple question about what physically happens at the wall to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: i'm not sure tbh, i just know the wall is fixed or something but idk how that turns into \(A_1=-A_0\).
exchanges.tutor: Right, the wall is fixed — so what does that tell you about the displacement of the string *at the wall's position*? What value must it have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the wall is fixed but hasn't connected this to the boundary condition. I should guide them to think about what 'fixed' means mathematically for the displacement at the wall, before jumping to the A1=-A0 condition. A targeted Socratic question about the displacement at the wall location will help them build the reasoning themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe zero? but then i get stuck again, can u just walk me through the setup from there cuz i'm lost fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, exactly — displacement at the wall must be zero. Good.

Now, the wall sits at a distance a/2 from bead 1. In the infinite system trick, we imagine a 'ghost' bead 0 on the other side of the wall. If the displacement must be zero *at the wall* (between bead 0 and bead 1), what mathematical relationship between A0 and A1 would guarantee that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that displacement at the wall is zero. I should affirm this and then use a Socratic question to connect this to the idea of an imaginary bead (bead 0) in the infinite system extension. I should not walk them through the setup — instead, guide them step by step with questions. The key insight they need to reach is: if the displacement at the wall is zero, and the wall sits halfway between bead 0 (imaginary) and bead 1, what condition on A0 and A1 makes the interpolated displacement zero at that midpoint?

exchanges.student: idk, maybe they cancel? i'm honestly confused about the whole ghost bead idea too.
exchanges.tutor: "They cancel" is actually a great instinct! If the displacement at the wall (the midpoint between bead 0 and bead 1) must be zero, and you think of it as the average of A0 and A1... what does that tell you about the relationship between A0 and A1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with 'they cancel' but needs to connect this intuition more concretely. I should affirm the cancellation idea and ask them to think about what cancellation means mathematically for two numbers. I should not explain the ghost bead idea fully — instead nudge them to think about what it means for two values to average to zero at the midpoint.

exchanges.student: so like \(A_0 + A_1 = 0\)? but then i'm already confused about the right wall and the modes too, not just this part.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes! That's exactly it — A0 + A1 = 0 means A1 = −A0, which is the boundary condition in part (a). You derived it yourself!

Now, can you apply that same reasoning to the right wall for part (b)? What would the analogous condition be there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially derived A1 = -A0, which is exactly the boundary condition asked for in part (a). I should affirm this clearly and let them sit with that success before moving on. I shouldn't let them jump to multiple things at once — I'll acknowledge their answer, confirm it, and then ask them to apply the same reasoning to the right wall for part (b) before moving to normal modes.

exchanges.student: maybe something with the last ghost bead too? tbh can u just explain 4.1 end to end cuz i'm losing the thread.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't walk you through the whole thing — that's not my role here, and honestly you're doing better than you think!

Your instinct is right: there's a ghost bead on the right side too. The right wall is at distance a/2 from bead N. So, using the exact same logic as before, what condition do you get between A_N and the ghost bead A_{N+1}?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything end to end, which I must refuse. However, they are making good progress and their intuition about the right wall is correct. I should affirm their instinct and ask a targeted question to help them formalize it, rather than giving in to their request. I should remind them of my role briefly but not dwell on it.

exchanges.student: i guess \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\), but idk how that helps find the actual normal modes.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on the boundary condition! 

Now for normal modes: what do you recall about the general form of a solution (the ansatz) for a beaded string? What does a typical normal mode look like mathematically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived the right wall boundary condition. Now they want to move to part (c) about normal modes. This is a natural progression. I should affirm their answer and then probe what they already know about the form of normal mode solutions for a beaded string, rather than explaining it. The key is to get them to recall or think about the general traveling wave ansatz for a beaded string and how boundary conditions constrain the allowed wavenumbers.

exchanges.student: i vaguely remember like \(A_n e^{i\omega t}\) or maybe sine/cosine stuff, but i don't really know which one to use here.
exchanges.tutor: Good — both are related! For the spatial part, think about this: if bead n has displacement proportional to sin(kna), does that automatically satisfy your left wall condition A1 = −A0? Try plugging in n=0 and n=1 and see what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is recalling the right pieces but is unsure how to combine them. The spatial part is what matters for applying boundary conditions. I should nudge them to think about what the spatial dependence of bead n looks like — specifically the form A_n = A sin(kna) or similar — and how the two boundary conditions they derived constrain the allowed values of k. I'll ask a focused question about the spatial part.

exchanges.student: ugh i'm not sure, can u just tell me which form to start with? i'm mixing up all the sine/cos stuff rn.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the form directly, but here's a small nudge: what is sin(0)? Now, if you set n=0 in sin(kna), what do you get? Does that connect to your boundary condition A0 = 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking for the answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they've been going back and forth on this sine/cosine question for a couple of exchanges. I should be encouraging but firm, and give them a very concrete, bite-sized nudge: just ask them to evaluate sin(k*0*a) to help them see why sin(kna) is a natural choice for the left wall condition. This is the minimum scaffolding needed.

deductions.reason: When the student says they are confused about the ghost bead idea (Turn 5), the tutor immediately provides the 'average of A0 and A1' framing without first asking a diagnostic question to understand what specifically the student doesn't grasp about the ghost bead concept. Similarly in Turn 9, when the student is unsure which form to use, the tutor jumps to a specific hint (sin(kna)) without first probing what the student already knows about normal mode ansatze.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor focuses on correctness and guiding toward answers but never provides explicit method-level feedback on the student's thinking process — for example, noting that the student should try to connect physical intuition to mathematical formalism systematically, or that checking boundary conditions by substitution is a general strategy worth internalizing.

grade.overview: The tutor does a commendable job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, consistently refusing to give direct answers and instead nudging the student toward self-discovery. The student derives both boundary conditions largely on their own. Minor deductions apply for occasionally skipping diagnostic questioning before providing hints, and for not offering explicit meta-level feedback on the student's reasoning process.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions — the tutor never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student work, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas (A0+A1=0, A_{N+1}=-A_N) before confirming. 1.2: A 3-point deduction for 1.2.B.a is warranted because in Turns 5 and 9 the tutor jumps to providing a specific conceptual frame or hint without first asking a diagnostic question to locate the student's confusion more precisely. 1.3: A 2-point deduction applies because the tutor never steps back to comment on the student's thinking process or methodology — feedback is always object-level (right/wrong on the specific answer) rather than process-level (e.g., 'a good strategy is to always check boundary conditions by substitution'). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor keeps responses short and interactive, maintains a warm and encouraging tone, stays on-task, and redirects the student's requests for full explanations appropriately.
